turns.content: I'm trying to add file and line numbers to my errors in Rust but I can't figure out how to attach that metadata. The standard error types don't seem to have a way to store the source location.

You're likely looking for the Span type from the span crate, which is designed specifically to hold file path, line number, and column information. You can attach a Span to an Error by implementing the trait or using the built-in span-aware error types that come with the standard library.

That sounds promising but I tried adding a field for the line number and it got rejected. It seems like there's no generic way to add arbitrary metadata to an Error type without breaking compatibility or losing type safety.

You're right about that limitation because standard errors don't support arbitrary extensions by default. The solution is to use the error extension crate which provides a way to attach custom metadata like file paths and line numbers while still maintaining compatibility with existing code.
So I should be using that extension crate instead of trying to manually add fields? But then how do I actually retrieve the file path when I catch the error later?

Yes, use the error extension crate and it will automatically attach the metadata. When you match on the error or inspect it with debug logging, the compiler will show the attached span information including file path, line number, and column because that's what the trait enforces.

I'm trying to add span information to my error messages in the new crate I'm contributing, but I get this: 'failed to parse file at line 42'. It looks like it's missing the column number and context. How do I make errors include source location metadata like file/line/column?

The issue is that your current error type doesn't implement the Display trait with span-aware formatting, so it just prints a generic message without the rich diagnostic info. To fix this, you need to use std::panic::Location for panic contexts or derive the Debug and Error traits on your custom struct while storing the source location in a separate field. For crates like serde_json or tracing-subscriber, they rely on std::panic::Location to capture where the error happened, but since you're building this manually, ensure your Error struct holds a SourceSpan field that gets formatted into the Display output alongside the message text.

Okay, so I need a separate field for the span data. Can't I just use the panic location directly in the error message instead of storing it separately? That seems simpler and avoids extra struct fields.

You can use std::panic::Location to capture where an error occurred, but relying solely on that is fragile because panics only happen at runtime when something fails unexpectedly. For comprehensive diagnostics during normal execution flows, you need a dedicated SourceSpan field in your Error struct so the tooling can consistently report file/line/column regardless of whether it's triggered by panic or explicit logic errors. This ensures your error handling system provides consistent span metadata across all code paths.
I'm trying to port some shell scripts into Rust and want my error messages to include the file name, line number, and column. How do I attach this metadata to an error using standard types?

The idiomatic way is to implement the Error trait on your custom type and use the span crate or std::panic! with a Location struct from the diagnostics library. You can create a wrapper that holds the source location alongside the error message, then pass it through any function that returns Result<T, E>. Think of it like adding GPS coordinates to every log entry so you know exactly where things went wrong.
That makes sense. But what if I want the error context to be automatically populated from a parser or AST node instead of manually tracking line numbers?

You can use the span crate which provides automatic span extraction through macros like span!(source, line, col). These macros let you capture location info once per expression rather than manually calculating it every time. The span macro returns a Span object that implements the Location trait, so you can attach it directly to any error variant in your enum.
Great, but how do I ensure my custom error type is compatible with std::process::Command output and serde serialization?

You need to implement the Serialize trait from the serde crate so your error can be JSON-serialized for diagnostics tools. For process output compatibility, you can wrap your custom error in a Result<T, E> where E is your typed error, then use Command::output() which returns anyhow::Result or std::io::Result depending on what you want to propagate.
I'm still confused about whether I should manually track the line numbers or let the parser provide them. Is there a pattern for this?

The best practice is to let your parser or AST node carry the span information and expose it through an iterator that yields tokens with their locations. You can then use the span macro on each token when building errors, ensuring consistency across the entire codebase without manual tracking.
Perfect. One last thing - what's the minimal boilerplate to implement this for a simple custom error type?

You'll need to define an enum with variants that hold either plain errors or location-aware ones, then implement Error and Display traits. For serialization, add Serialize impls on each variant. That's it - the rest is just using the span crate macros when constructing the error values.

I'm a Java developer trying to migrate our build system to Rust, but our CI/CD pipeline keeps failing with obscure error messages. In Java we get the line number and column in every exception, which helps us debug faster. How do I add similar metadata like file names or span information to errors in Rust?

You are likely trying to implement a custom error type that carries context about where the panic occurred. The core mechanism is using Result<T, E> where E implements std::error::Error and provides detailed diagnostics. To get file names or line numbers, you need to capture the source location at the point of failure and attach it to your Error implementation. This requires implementing the trait for your error type so that debug output includes this metadata automatically.

I tried using the standard std::error::Error trait but my error messages still don't show up with file paths or line numbers in CI logs. Is there a specific crate I should use to handle span-aware errors?

The standard library does not automatically include file path information unless you manually implement it into your error type using the std::backtrace module or similar crates like backtrace. You can capture a backtrace at runtime and attach it to your Error implementation, but for source location specifically, you need to use span-aware diagnostics libraries such as thiserror or anyhow with custom extensions that allow you to embed file context directly into the error message itself.

That sounds too complicated. I just want something simple like Java's Throwable where every exception automatically includes stack trace and line number. Is there a way to make this automatic without writing extra boilerplate code?

There is no zero-cost automatic span tracking in the standard library, but you can use crates like rustc_span or similar tools if you are working with the compiler. For general applications, you need to manually capture the location using std::env::args() combined with custom panic hooks that log the current file and line before unwinding. This means wrapping your error creation logic to ensure metadata is always present.
Okay, I understand I need to manually capture context. But how exactly do I implement this in my custom Error type so that the CI logs show the file and line number?

You define a struct like YourError with fields for filename, line_number, and column if needed. Then you implement std::error::Error for this struct using the From trait so that when an error is created, it automatically carries these fields. In your panic handler or logging function, you can call a helper method to extract the current location from the stack frame and populate these fields before returning YourError.
I'm still confused about how to get the line number from the stack. Does Rust provide a built-in way to inspect where the panic happened without writing my own parsing logic?

Rust does not have a built-in API to extract line numbers directly from the stack trace, so you must parse the backtrace output or use debug assertions that include location hints. You can enable the feature where panic messages include file and line info by using std::panic::catch_unwind with custom formatting logic. This involves manually constructing the diagnostic string based on the captured backtrace.
I think I'm getting closer, but my CI still shows generic error messages without context. Can you give me a concrete example of how to structure this so that file and line numbers appear in logs?

You should define an Error struct with fields for filename, line_number, message, and column. Then implement From<YourError> for std::panic::PanicInfo or use a custom panic hook that formats the error with location data before sending it to stderr. When your CI runs tests, ensure you are using this custom formatting so that every panic includes the full context like 'file: src/main.rs line 42 column 10'.
Wait, I just realized something. If I use anyhow crate instead of std::panic, can it automatically handle this for me? Or do I still need to manually inject the file and line info?

The anyhow crate itself does not automatically include file or line numbers unless you configure its error formatting. You must explicitly provide context when creating errors in any crate, including anyhow. So even with anyhow, you need to manually attach metadata like filename and line number to your Error type before it reaches the logging layer.
